title: Italian Game
shortDescription: A classical 1.e4 e5 opening beginning with 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4, combining rapid development, pressure on f7, positional maneuvering, and sharp gambits.

root.comment: White occupies the center and opens lines for the queen and bishop.

children.comment: The knight develops with tempo against e5.
The Italian bishop targets f7.
The quiet d3 setup keeps the center flexible.
c3 prepares d4 and gives the bishop a retreat square on c2.
Re1 supports e4 and prepares a later d4 break.

line.name: Giuoco Pianissimo: Basic Setup
line.description: White builds a quiet Italian center with d3 and prepares c3, Re1, and Nbd2.

children.comment: ...a6 prepares ...Ba7 and sometimes ...b5.
Bb3 keeps pressure on f7.
h3 prevents ...Bg4 and gives the king luft.

line.name: Giuoco Pianissimo: Ba7 and h3 Setup
line.description: A standard modern structure with c3, Re1, h3, and a bishop retreat to preserve the Italian bishop.

children.comment: Nf1 prepares Ng3 and frees d2.
Nxe5 uses the tension created by ...d5.
d4 finally opens the center after White has completed development.

line.name: Giuoco Pianissimo: Prepared d4 Break
line.description: White completes development and only then strikes with d4 under favorable circumstances.

children.comment: a4 limits ...b5 and gives White useful queenside space.

line.name: Giuoco Pianissimo: Queenside Expansion
line.description: White combines the standard knight maneuver with a4 to restrain Black’s queenside play.

children.comment: Nbd2 begins the standard maneuver.
Nf1 reroutes the knight without blocking the c-pawn.
Ng3 increases pressure on f5 and supports a kingside attack.

line.name: Giuoco Pianissimo: Nbd2-f1-g3
line.description: White executes the classic knight maneuver toward g3, strengthening the kingside attack.

children.comment: ...a6 prepares queenside expansion.
a4 restrains ...b5.
Re1 supports e4.

line.name: Giuoco Pianissimo: Early a4
line.description: White uses a4 to slow ...b5 and secure the bishop on a2 or b3.

children.comment: h3 prevents ...Bg4 and prepares a slow kingside buildup.
Nbd2 begins the Nf1-g3 maneuver.
Nf1 improves the knight while keeping the center flexible.
Bc2 preserves the bishop for kingside pressure.
...d5 is Black’s thematic central break.

line.name: Giuoco Pianissimo: Modern Main Line
line.description: A deeper modern Italian sequence featuring restrained center play, prophylaxis, and piece maneuvering.

children.comment: c3 prepares the central pawn break d4.
White opens the center while ahead in space.
White rebuilds a strong pawn center.
The knight recaptures and develops toward the center.

line.name: Giuoco Piano: Classical Center
line.description: White immediately prepares d4 and challenges Black in the center.

children.comment: Nc3 develops while blocking the check.
Black grabs the e4 pawn.
White castles and accelerates development.
d5 drives pieces away and opens attacking lines.
Re1 brings the last major piece into the center.

line.name: Giuoco Piano: Møller Attack
line.description: White sacrifices central material for rapid development and an attack against the uncastled king.

children.comment: Rxe4 recovers the piece with active play.

line.name: Møller Attack: Main Tactical Line
line.description: A forcing continuation where White uses d5 and Re1 to exploit Black’s lagging king.

children.comment: e5 gains space and attacks the knight.
Black counters in the center immediately.
Bb5 increases pressure on c6.
White restores the pawn center.

line.name: Giuoco Piano: Center Attack with e5
line.description: White advances e5 to chase the f6-knight after opening the center.

children.comment: White makes the king safe immediately.
d3 protects e4 and keeps the position compact.
c3 prepares d4.
Re1 reinforces e4.

line.name: Giuoco Pianissimo: Early Castling
line.description: A simple move order where White castles before deciding on c3 and d4.

children.comment: d4 offers the Deutz Gambit.
e5 drives the knight and opens lines.
Re1+ uses the open e-file immediately.

line.name: Italian Game: Deutz Gambit
line.description: White delays c3 and offers the d-pawn for rapid development and central activity.

children.comment: b4 offers a pawn to deflect the bishop.
c3 attacks the bishop and prepares d4.
d4 opens the center before Black can consolidate.
White castles and brings the rook toward the open center.
White restores the central pawn mass.

line.name: Evans Gambit Accepted: Basic Development
line.description: White sacrifices the b-pawn to gain tempi and build a powerful center.

children.comment: Nc3 adds pressure on d5 and supports development.

line.name: Evans Gambit Accepted: Compromised Defense
line.description: Black retreats to a5; White builds the classical center and develops with tempo.

children.comment: Qb3 attacks f7 and b7 at once.

line.name: Evans Gambit Accepted: Main Line Attack
line.description: White builds the ideal center, castles, and places the queen on b3 to pressure f7 and b7.

children.comment: Qa4 develops the queen with pressure on c6.
Qb3 renews threats against f7 and b7.

line.name: Evans Gambit: Stone-Ware Variation
line.description: White uses Ba3 to interfere with Black’s castling and reinforce pressure on the dark squares.

children.comment: ...d6 is a solid way to return material and reduce White’s attack.
White exchanges queens to recover material safely.
Nxe5 wins back the central pawn.

line.name: Evans Gambit: Lasker Defense
line.description: Black returns material with ...d6 and ...Bb6 to blunt White’s initiative; White keeps active piece play.

children.comment: Black declines and keeps the bishop on the diagonal.
a4 gains more space and threatens a5.
White develops naturally and keeps attacking chances.

line.name: Evans Gambit Declined
line.description: Black refuses the pawn, so White gains queenside space without sacrificing material.

children.comment: d4 immediately challenges Black’s e5 pawn.
White castles instead of recapturing, prioritizing development.
e5 attacks the knight and opens tactical lines.
White takes on f6 and damages Black’s kingside.
Re1+ forces Black to respond to the open e-file.
Ng5 increases pressure on e6 and f7.

line.name: Italian Gambit: Max Lange Attack
line.description: White opens the center with d4 and uses e5 to drive the knight while attacking Black’s king.

children.comment: Nc3 adds development with tempo.
Nce4 centralizes another piece and attacks the queen.

line.name: Max Lange Attack: Main Line
line.description: A sharp theoretical sequence where White sacrifices material to keep Black’s king in the center.

children.comment: The Two Knights Defense attacks e4.
d3 calmly defends the pawn.
c3 prepares a later d4 expansion.

line.name: Two Knights Defense: Quiet d3
line.description: White avoids forcing tactics and enters a solid modern Italian structure.

children.comment: Ng5 creates the immediate threat Nxf7.
Black strikes in the center—the critical response.
White accepts the challenge by taking on d5.
Na5 attacks the bishop and avoids the Fried Liver sacrifice.
The check disrupts Black before the bishop retreats.
White usually returns the bishop to e2 and prepares to castle.

line.name: Two Knights Defense: Main Line with 5...Na5
line.description: White attacks f7, then retreats the bishop and keeps the extra pawn after Black gains development.

children.comment: ...h6 drives away the active knight.
Ne5 centralizes the knight and attacks c6 and f7.

line.name: Two Knights Defense: 5...Na5 Main Line
line.description: The theoretical main line where White keeps an extra pawn but Black gains activity and development.

children.comment: Nxd5 allows the famous sacrifice.
Nxf7 forks the queen and rook and forces the king to capture.
Qf3+ brings the queen into the attack with tempo.
Nc3 adds another attacker to d5 and e4.

line.name: Two Knights Defense: Fried Liver Attack
line.description: White sacrifices a knight on f7 to drag the king into the center.

children.comment: Qe4 keeps the king boxed in and supports d4.
d4 opens the center while Black’s king remains exposed.

line.name: Fried Liver Attack: Main Line with Ke6
line.description: The classic forcing attack continues with Nc3, d4, and Re1 against the exposed king.

children.comment: ...b5 is the Ulvestad Variation, gaining tempo on the bishop.
Bf1 is the most resilient retreat.
Ne4 attacks c5 and prepares active development.

line.name: Two Knights Defense: Ulvestad Variation
line.description: Black uses ...b5 to attack the bishop and create tactical counterplay instead of recapturing on d5.

children.comment: ...Nd4 is the Fritz Variation.
c3 attacks the advanced knight.
...b5 gains another tempo against the bishop.
Bf1 keeps the bishop safe.
Ne4 centralizes and attacks c5.

line.name: Two Knights Defense: Fritz Variation
line.description: Black plays ...Nd4, creating threats against c2 and forcing White to react accurately.

children.comment: ...Bc5 ignores f7 and launches the Traxler Counterattack.
Bxf7+ is the principled forcing response.
Bb3 preserves the bishop after drawing the king forward.
d3 opens the dark-squared bishop and supports e4.
Nf3 completes development and prepares castling.

line.name: Traxler Counterattack: 5.Bxf7+
line.description: White answers the dangerous Traxler with a forcing check on f7 and keeps the king under pressure.

children.comment: Nf3 develops and covers key central squares.
Nh3 avoids tactical forks and protects f2.

line.name: Traxler Counterattack: King Walk Line
line.description: White accepts the tactical challenge and navigates a forcing king-safety line after ...Bxf2+.

children.comment: The Hungarian Defense is solid but passive.
White claims the center immediately.
Re1 supports e4 and prepares expansion.
c3 strengthens the d4 center.

line.name: Hungarian Defense: Central Expansion
line.description: Against 3...Be7, White takes space with d4 and develops normally.

children.comment: Black offers the dubious Blackburne Shilling Gambit.
Nxd4 is the clean response—remove the advanced knight.
White castles instead of chasing material.

line.name: Blackburne Shilling Gambit: Safe Refutation
line.description: White avoids the famous trap by exchanging the centralized knight.

children.comment: ...f5 is the aggressive Rousseau Gambit.
d4 opens the center while Black’s king is still exposed.
White castles and increases pressure on the center.
Ng5 creates threats against e4 and f7.
Nxe4 regains the pawn with active pieces.

line.name: Rousseau Gambit: Central Counter
line.description: White meets 3...f5 by striking in the center before Black can build a kingside attack.
